ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers spring rolls as a category. No specific code for Malaysian deep-fried version; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for fried spring rolls. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for this food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of fried spring rolls in Singapore and Malaysia consists of approximately 3 pieces, weighing around 150 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Spring Roll, Fried — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia / FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for fried spring rolls (popia goreng) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian fried pastry-wrapped snacks. The restaurant version served at Marina Seafood House is a Zi Char-style preparation consistent with the Malaysian fried spring roll profile. Per-100g values represent a typical deep-fried spring roll with mixed meat and vegetable filling.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spring Rolls and Analogous Deep-Fried Wheat Pastry Snacks — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malaysian fried spring rolls in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated by analogy with deep-fried wheat-pastry wrapped snacks with mixed vegetable and meat fillings, which cluster in the GI range 45–55 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; Foster-Powell K et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002). The fat and protein content of the filling moderates the glycemic response of the wheat wrapper. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 34.5) / 100 ≈ 17.9, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 23.0 × 1.5 = 34.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~570 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fried spring roll intake to one or two pieces per occasion, as a 150 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 570 mg sodium — roughly 25% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced-sodium dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~17.9 per serving) and refined wheat wrapper
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume fried spring rolls in moderation; the estimated GI of 52 (low) is partially offset by a moderate glycemic load of approximately 17.9 per 150 g serving due to the refined wheat pastry wrapper, and the deep-frying adds significant fat calories that may impair postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat; elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit fried spring roll consumption due to the combined effect of elevated total fat (~17.3 g per 150 g serving), saturated fat (~4.2 g per serving), and sodium (~570 mg per serving), all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried spring rolls (炸春卷; Popia Goreng) are a popular deep-fried appetiser and snack widely served in Malaysian and Singaporean cuisine, including Zi Char restaurants. They consist of a thin, crispy wheat-flour pastry wrapper encasing a savoury filling of minced pork or chicken, shredded cabbage, carrot, bean sprouts, glass noodles, and aromatics such as garlic, seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil. The rolls are deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp on the outside, yielding a crunchy shell with a moist, flavourful interior. Fried spring rolls are typically served as a starter or side dish, often accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or a vinegar-based dipping sauce. Per 100 g, they provide approximately 220 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 11.5 g fat, and 23 g carbohydrate, reflecting the energy-dense nature of deep-frying. The estimated glycemic index is 52 (low), though the glycemic load per standard 150 g serving is moderate at approximately 17.9, owing to the refined wheat wrapper. Sodium content is notable at approximately 380 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the moderate glycemic load and high fat content from deep-frying warrant careful portion management. Fried spring rolls are not suitable for vegetarians or vegans when meat-filled, and contain gluten and soy allergens. They are a culturally significant dish across Malaysia, Singapore, and the broader Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried spring rolls?
What is the GI of spring rolls?
Are spring rolls high in fat?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for fried spring rolls (popia goreng), which represent the closest approved primary source for this Malaysian Zi Char preparation. No exact match for this specific restaurant dish exists in any approved database. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework using comparable deep-fried wheat-pastry snacks. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source databases for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this food. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting trace levels from deep-frying in commercial vegetable oils; this is a plausible low-level value consistent with the cooking method. The food is listed under cuisine tag 'Crab' in the field survey source data, which likely reflects the restaurant's broader menu category (Marina Seafood House — Boat Quay) rather than the spring roll filling itself; the spring roll filling is assumed to be the standard pork/vegetable variety unless otherwise specified. Halal status is marked false as pork is a common ingredient in Malaysian Chinese-style fried spring rolls; halal variants exist but cannot be confirmed for this specific restaurant without verification. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. GL per serving = (52 × 34.5) / 100 = 17.9, where 34.5 g carbohydrate is derived from 23.0 g per 100 g × 1.5 (150 g serving).
